---
title: "CB3 Equity Report — Housing & Affordability Maps"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: cb3
---

## Purpose

This notebook reads the clean Housing & Affordability tract table and maps seven
representative dimensions:

1. Severe rent burden: `severe_rent_burden_pct`
2. Crowded housing: `crowded_households_pct`
3. Subsidized properties at risk (2025–2030): `subsidized_units_expiring_2025_2030`
4. Subsidized properties at risk (2031–2040): `subsidized_units_expiring_2031_2040`
5. New affordable housing: `new_affordable_units_since_2018`
6. New affordable housing by target AMI band: `new_affordable_extremely_low_income_units`
   and four other AMI-tier unit counts
7. Housing quality: `hpd_open_class_b`, `hpd_open_class_c`

Most metrics are presented on their own map, as a bubble layer sized by
magnitude over selectable demographic choropleth base layers. Severe rent
burden and crowded housing are only ever reported at the tract level, so
their bubbles sit at each tract's centroid. Subsidized properties at risk and
new affordable housing are building-level data, so their bubbles use each
building's own coordinates instead of a tract centroid. New affordable
housing by AMI band and housing quality instead use one donut marker per
point (sized by total, wedges by category share), since their categories
commonly co-occur at the same point and would otherwise need several
same-coordinate bubble layers that stack and hide each other. Map HTML files
are written to `maps/housing_and_affordability/`.

The notebook also reads the clean CB3 tract baseline profile and creates one
selectable-layer demographics map for median household income, age, race and
ethnicity, poverty, and limited-English-proficient households.

```{python}
# Import packages for tract data, geometry, classification, and interactive maps.
from pathlib import Path
import sys
import yaml

import folium
import geopandas as gpd
import pandas as pd

# Ensure the reusable scripts package imports whether this QMD is rendered from
# the project root or from scripts/.
for candidate in [Path.cwd(), *Path.cwd().parents]:
    if (candidate / "src" / "map_utils.py").exists():
        sys.path.insert(0, str(candidate))
        break

from src.map_utils import (
    add_map_title,
    add_metric_layer,
    add_zero_value_legend,
    build_grouped_bubble_map,
    build_metric_bubble_map,
    find_project_dir,
    make_base_map,
)
```

```{python}
# Define project inputs and output locations. Resolve the project root so the
# notebook works whether it is rendered from the root folder or from scripts/.
PROJECT_DIR = find_project_dir()
CLEAN_DATA_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "housing_and_affordability_tract.csv"
)
BASELINE_PROFILE_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "cb3_tract_baseline_profile_clean.csv"
)
SUBSIDIZED_POINTS_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "housing_and_affordability_subsidized_points.csv"
)
NEW_CONSTRUCTION_POINTS_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "clean" / "housing_and_affordability_new_construction_points.csv"
)
TRACT_GEOMETRY_PATH = (
    PROJECT_DIR / "data" / "raw" / "Geography" / "2020_Census_Tracts_20260505.geojson"
)
MAP_OUTPUT_DIR = PROJECT_DIR / "maps" / "housing_and_affordability"
MAP_OUTPUT_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# metadata schema for the colors and units for each metric
with open(PROJECT_DIR / "docs" / "metric_metadata.yml") as f:
    ALL_METRICS = yaml.safe_load(f)
```

## Prepare tract geometry and metrics

```{python}
# Read the clean metrics with GEOID preserved as an 11-character string.
metrics = pd.read_csv(CLEAN_DATA_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})

# Read the 2020 tract polygons, normalize GEOID, and retain only the 31 clean rows.
tract_geometry = gpd.read_file(TRACT_GEOMETRY_PATH)
tract_geometry["GEOID"] = tract_geometry["geoid"].astype("string").str.zfill(11)
tract_geometry = tract_geometry[["GEOID", "geometry"]]

# Join the metrics to polygons and verify a complete one-to-one match.
tracts = tract_geometry.merge(
    metrics,
    on="GEOID",
    how="inner",
    validate="one_to_one",
)
tracts = gpd.GeoDataFrame(tracts, geometry="geometry", crs=tract_geometry.crs)

assert len(tracts) == 31
assert tracts["GEOID"].is_unique
assert set(tracts["GEOID"]) == set(metrics["GEOID"])

# Read tract-level demographics and join them to the same 31 tract polygons.
# The demographics file currently has 30 tracts, so one tract remains mapped
# as "Not available" in demographic layers.
baseline_profile = pd.read_csv(BASELINE_PROFILE_PATH, dtype={"geoid": "string"})
baseline_profile["GEOID"] = baseline_profile["geoid"].str.zfill(11)

DEMOGRAPHIC_COLUMNS = [
    "GEOID",
    "median_household_income",
    "age_0_to_19_share",
    "age_20_to_64_share",
    "age_65_plus_share",
    "white_non_hispanic_share",
    "black_non_hispanic_share",
    "asian_non_hispanic_share",
    "hispanic_share",
    "poverty_rate",
    "lep_household_share",
]

tracts = tracts.merge(
    baseline_profile[DEMOGRAPHIC_COLUMNS],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="one_to_one",
)

# Read building-level subsidized-housing-at-risk points (native lat/lon, not
# collapsed to a tract centroid) and attach tract/NTA labels for tooltips.
subsidized_points = pd.read_csv(SUBSIDIZED_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
subsidized_points = subsidized_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)

# Read building-level new-construction-since-2018 points (native lat/lon, not
# collapsed to a tract centroid) and attach tract/NTA labels for tooltips.
new_construction_points = pd.read_csv(NEW_CONSTRUCTION_POINTS_PATH, dtype={"GEOID": "string"})
new_construction_points = new_construction_points.merge(
    tracts[["GEOID", "tract_label", "nta_name"]],
    on="GEOID",
    how="left",
    validate="many_to_one",
)
```

```{python}
# Build METRIC_SPECS and DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS from metric_metadata.yml.
# To change a palette, label, or unit, edit the YAML — not this notebook.
#
# `palette` (and positive_values_only/zero_color/zero_label) are only
# consumed by add_metric_layer() for choropleths — i.e. only demographic
# entries actually need them now. Domain metrics render as bubbles via
# build_metric_bubble_map()/build_grouped_bubble_map(), which don't read
# these fields, so they're optional here rather than required.
def _to_map_spec(meta, include_filename=False):
    spec = {
        "dimension": meta["short_label"],
        "label":     meta["label"],
        "unit":      meta["unit"],
    }
    if include_filename:
        spec["filename"] = meta["filename"]
    for key in ("palette", "positive_values_only", "zero_color", "zero_label"):
        if key in meta:
            spec[key] = meta[key]
    return spec

METRIC_SPECS = {
    var: _to_map_spec(meta, include_filename=True)
    for var, meta in ALL_METRICS.items()
    if meta["domain"] == "housing_and_affordability"
}


# senior_poverty_rate is registered as a demographic metric for other domains'
# notebooks, but is not featured as a layer on the housing demographic maps.
EXCLUDED_DEMOGRAPHIC_METRICS = {"senior_poverty_rate"}

DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS = {
    var: _to_map_spec(meta)
    for var, meta in ALL_METRICS.items()
    if meta["domain"] == "demographics" and var not in EXCLUDED_DEMOGRAPHIC_METRICS
}

for metric in METRIC_SPECS:
    assert metric in tracts.columns, f"{metric} missing from tracts"
for metric in DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS:
    assert metric in tracts.columns, f"{metric} missing from tracts"
```

```{python}
# Reuse shared mapping helpers from scripts/map_utils.py. Keep this small wrapper
# so the rest of the notebook can call base_map() without passing tracts each time.
def base_map():
    return make_base_map(tracts)

```

```{python}
# Thin, housing-domain-specific wrappers around the generic map builders in
# src/map_utils.py, so call sites below don't have to repeat tracts/
# METRIC_SPECS/DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS/MAP_OUTPUT_DIR on every call.
def create_single_metric_map(metric, **kwargs):
    spec = METRIC_SPECS[metric]
    return build_metric_bubble_map(
        tracts, METRIC_SPECS, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS, metric,
        title=f"CB3 Housing & Affordability: {spec['dimension']}",
        output_path=MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / spec["filename"],
        **kwargs,
    )


def create_grouped_metric_map(metrics, filename, title, subtitle, **kwargs):
    return build_grouped_bubble_map(
        tracts, METRIC_SPECS, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS, metrics,
        title=title, subtitle=subtitle,
        output_path=MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / filename,
        **kwargs,
    )
```

## Separate maps

### Severe rent burden

```{python}
severe_rent_burden_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "severe_rent_burden_pct"
)
severe_rent_burden_map
```

### Crowded housing

```{python}
crowded_housing_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "crowded_households_pct"
)
crowded_housing_map
```

### Subsidized properties at risk (2025–2030)

```{python}
# Subsidized housing is reported at the building level, not the tract level,
# so bubbles use each building's own coordinates rather than a tract centroid.
subsidized_housing_at_risk_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "subsidized_units_expiring_2025_2030",
    points=subsidized_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
)
subsidized_housing_at_risk_map
```

### Subsidized properties at risk (2031–2040)

```{python}
subsidized_housing_at_risk_2031_2040_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "subsidized_units_expiring_2031_2040",
    points=subsidized_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
)
subsidized_housing_at_risk_2031_2040_map
```

### New affordable housing

```{python}
# New construction is reported at the building level, not the tract level, so
# bubbles use each building's own coordinates rather than a tract centroid.
new_affordable_housing_map = create_single_metric_map(
    "new_affordable_units_since_2018",
    points=new_construction_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
)
new_affordable_housing_map
```

### New affordable housing by target AMI band

```{python}
# A building's units commonly span more than one AMI band, so five separate
# same-coordinate bubble layers would stack and hide each other. Instead,
# combine_as_pie=True draws one donut marker per building, sized by its total
# income-restricted units and split into wedges by AMI band. AMI bands are an
# ordered tier, not unrelated categories, so wedges use a single-hue
# black-to-light-grey ordinal ramp (validated: monotone lightness, visible
# step gaps, light end clears contrast) rather than distinct hues. Order
# controls wedge color, so the deepest-affordability band (0-30% AMI) is
# listed first for the darkest, most prominent shade.
AMI_BAND_COLORS = ["#1a1a1a", "#404040", "#616161", "#828282", "#a3a3a3"]
new_affordable_housing_by_ami_output_path = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / "new_affordable_housing_by_ami.html"
new_affordable_housing_by_ami_map = create_grouped_metric_map(
    [
        "new_affordable_extremely_low_income_units",
        "new_affordable_very_low_income_units",
        "new_affordable_low_income_units",
        "new_affordable_moderate_income_units",
        "new_affordable_middle_income_units",
    ],
    new_affordable_housing_by_ami_output_path.name,
    "CB3 Housing & Affordability: New affordable housing by target AMI band",
    "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. Donut "
    "markers: size shows total new-construction (since 2018) income-restricted "
    "units at each building; wedges show its mix of target AMI bands.",
    points=new_construction_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
    combine_as_pie=True,
    pie_name="New affordable housing by target AMI band",
    pie_legend_title="New construction by AMI band",
    pie_colors=AMI_BAND_COLORS,
)
new_affordable_housing_by_ami_map
```

### Housing quality

```{python}
# One donut marker per tract centroid, sized by total open HPD Class B/C
# violations, split into wedges by severity share (Class B hazardous, Class C
# immediately hazardous) rather than three separate maps (total, Class B
# only, Class C only). Wedge colors are two grayscale shades rather than
# distinct hues, with Class C (the more severe, immediately hazardous class)
# given the darker, more prominent shade.
HOUSING_QUALITY_COLORS = ["#cccccc", "#1a1a1a"]
housing_quality_output_path = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / "housing_quality.html"
housing_quality_map = create_grouped_metric_map(
    ["hpd_open_class_b", "hpd_open_class_c"],
    housing_quality_output_path.name,
    "CB3 Housing & Affordability: Housing quality",
    "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. Donut "
    "markers: size shows total open HPD violations at each tract; wedges "
    "show its mix of Class B (hazardous) and Class C (immediately hazardous).",
    combine_as_pie=True,
    pie_name="Housing quality (open HPD violations)",
    pie_legend_title="Violation class",
    pie_colors=HOUSING_QUALITY_COLORS,
)
housing_quality_map
```

## Demographic context map

```{python}
# Add demographic context as selectable layers. The missing tract in the
# baseline profile is retained and shown in gray as "Not available".
demographics_map = base_map()
for index, metric in enumerate(DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS):
    add_metric_layer(
        demographics_map, tracts, metric, DEMOGRAPHIC_SPECS[metric],
        show=index == 0, add_legend=False, overlay=False,
    )

add_map_title(
    demographics_map,
    "CB3 tract baseline demographics",
    "Use the layer control in the upper-right to select income, age, race/ethnicity, poverty, or limited-English-proficient household layers.",
)
add_zero_value_legend(
    demographics_map,
    "#d9d9d9",
    "Demographic data not available",
)
folium.LayerControl(collapsed=False, position="topright").add_to(demographics_map)

demographics_output_path = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / "tract_baseline_demographics_layers.html"
demographics_map.save(demographics_output_path)
demographics_map
```

## Demographics with subsidized housing at risk (point layer)

```{python}
# Subsidized housing is reported at the building level, not the tract level,
# so bubbles use each building's own coordinates rather than a tract centroid.
demographic_bubble_output_path = MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / "subsidized_housing_at_risk_2025_2040.html"
demographic_bubble_map = create_grouped_metric_map(
    ["subsidized_units_expiring_2025_2030", "subsidized_units_expiring_2031_2040"],
    demographic_bubble_output_path.name,
    "CB3 demographics with subsidized housing at risk",
    "Choropleth: use the layer control to pick a demographic fill. Bubbles: "
    "size shows subsidized units at risk at each building, split into two "
    "selectable layers by expiration window (2025-2030, 2031-2040).",
    points=subsidized_points,
    lat_col="latitude",
    lon_col="longitude",
    tooltip_fields=["standard_address", "tract_label"],
    tooltip_aliases=["Address", "Census tract"],
)
demographic_bubble_map
```

## Output validation

```{python}
# Verify that all HTML deliverables were written.
expected_map_files = [
    MAP_OUTPUT_DIR / spec["filename"] for spec in METRIC_SPECS.values()
] + [
    demographics_output_path,
    demographic_bubble_output_path,
]

for map_file in expected_map_files:
    assert map_file.exists()
    assert map_file.stat().st_size > 10_000

print(f"Wrote {len(expected_map_files)} map files to {MAP_OUTPUT_DIR}")
```
